architecture.summary: SoftSAE is a sparse autoencoder that replaces fixed sparsity constraints with adaptive, input-dependent sparsity selection. A Dynamic Sparsity MLP module predicts a continuous sparsity level k for each input based on its complexity. During training, a differentiable Soft Top-K operator applies soft selection weights to latent activations; at inference, hard Top-K selection with discretized k is used. The selected sparse features are reconstructed via a standard decoder. Information flows from input through encoder, through the Dynamic Sparsity MLP (which produces k), through Soft/Hard Top-K selection, and through decoder for reconstruction.

biblio.title: SoftSAE: Dynamic Top-K Selection for Adaptive Sparse Autoencoders

claims.statement: SoftSAE achieves a superior balance between reconstruction fidelity and interpretability, uncovering monosemantic features that more faithfully reflect the multiscale complexity of natural data
On CLIP embeddings, SoftSAE matches the reconstruction–sparsity performance of TopK and BatchTopK, slightly outperforming BatchTopK at comparable error while remaining close to TopK
On Gemma-2-2B activations, SoftSAE performs comparably to Matryoshka, with a slight advantage in reconstruction at similar sparsity levels
Using SAE Bench absorption and splitting metrics, SoftSAE shows a clear advantage in reducing feature absorption at higher sparsity levels and performs comparably to state-of-the-art Matryoshka SAEs

On Spurious Correlation Removal (SCR) and Targeted Probe Perturbation (TPP) metrics, SoftSAE outperforms TopK and BatchTopK on SCR, and matches or surpasses Matryoshka on TPP at higher sparsity levels

The Dynamic Sparsity MLP learns to assign low complexity scores to simple inputs (e.g., images with uniform background and single entity) and high scores to complex inputs (multiple entities, rich backgrounds, or text). For text, the estimated complexity increases with the amount of context and knowledge required to capture meaning
For CLIP SAE training, SoftSAE required approximately 2 hours on a single Nvidia GH200 120GB GPU for 40k steps, compared to 1.5 hours for baseline methods
SoftSAE incurs higher computational cost than baseline methods, primarily due to the differentiable Soft Top-K operator, which becomes a bottleneck for large dictionaries and may limit scalability
